---
title: "Python Tip: the `in` Operator"
subtitle: "Check if an object is in any collection with one keyword"
author: "Shep Bryan IV"
description: "Learn how to use the in operator to check membership in lists, strings, and dictionaries."
---



<!-- PANEL 1 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

:::{.center}

# Python Tip: the `in` Operator

Need to check if something is contained in a list?  
Want to see if a string contains a substring?  
Use the `in` operator for a clean, readable one-liner!  

:::{.python-logo}
:::

:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 2 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

## Checking Lists

Use `in` to check if a value exists in a list.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# Don't do this
found = False
for fruit in fruits:
    if fruit == "banana":
        found = True
print(found)

# Do this instead
print("banana" in fruits)
print("grape" in fruits)
```
:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 3 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

## Checking Strings

`in` also works on strings to check for substrings.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
message = "Hello, world!"

print("world" in message)
print("python" in message)
```
:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 4 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

## Checking Dictionaries

With dictionaries, `in` checks the **keys** by default.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
scores = {"alice": 95, "bob": 87, "carol": 92}
# Check keys
print("alice" in scores)
# Check values
print(95 in scores.values())
# Check pairs
print(("bob", 87) in scores.items())
```
:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 5 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

## Using `not in`

Flip the check with `not in` to test for absence.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
banned = ["spam", "ads", "clickbait"]

username = "spam"
if username not in banned:
    print(f"Welcome, {username}!")
else:
    print(f"{username} is banned.")
```
:::

:::



<!-- PANEL 6 -->
:::{.tutorial-panel}

:::{.center}

## Wrap-Up

Now you can use `in` and `not in` to check membership in lists strings, and dictionaries cleanly and concisely!

Follow me for more tips.  
Shep Bryan IV

:::

:::
